coredata.dc:title: Achieving the simultaneous improvement of degradation, thermal, and mechanical properties of polylactic acid composite films by carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Jianlong
Guo, Xinyuan
Tan, Rui
Huang, Mengde
Ren, Junchao
Liu, Weiwei
Wang, Mingfeng
Li, Bin
Ma, Zhong
Zhang, Qingfa
coredata.dc:description: Having porous structure, large surface area, and high carbon content of biochar facilitates interface bonding of polylactic acid (PLA) composites, but uneven dispersion by its irregular morphology is becoming a new challenge in damaging properties. Based on this, the novelty of this study is using carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to overcome the performance defects of caused PLA composites by biochar while the ultimate goal is to reveal the influence mechanism of CQDs on structure, characteristics, and properties of PLA composites based on disclosing the forming mechanism of CQDs. It was found that adding CQDs accelerated the degradation of PLA from the results of Phosphate Buffer Saline (PBS) degradation, hydrolysis, and soil degradation. PLA/CQDs composite films also showed better thermal properties due to the excellent thermal stability of CQDs, and nucleation effect of CQDs should be responsible for the improvement of PLA crystallization. Additionally, having good activity, regular morphology, and uniform size of CQDs facilitated uniform dispersion and good interface combination in PLA system and thereby improved the tensile strength, tensile modulus, and elongation at break simultaneously. As a comparison, the tensile strength, tensile modulus, and elongation at break of 1 wt% PLA/CQDs composite films are 55.00 MPa, 1.76 GPa, and 9.84 %, this provides a promising, sustainable, and eco-friendly solution for reinforcing PLA composites.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polylactic acid
Composite film
Carbon quantum dots
Property improvement

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the PLA/CQDs composite films
Degradation property of the composite films
Thermal stability analysis of the composite films
Tensile properties of the composite films

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Preparation process, (B) FTIR spectrum, (C) XRD pattern, (D) Raman spectrum, (E) TG and DTG curves, (F) UV–Visible spectrum, PL spectrum (Exi = 338 nm), PLE (Emi = 426 nm), and fluorescence image, (G) TEM image, (H) Synthesis mechanism of CQDs.
(A) Preparation process, (B) Samples images, (C) XRD patterns, (D) FTIR spectra, (E) SEM images, and (F) Interfacial mechanism of PLA composite films.

(A) Water contact angles, (B) Water absorption behavior, (C) PBS accelerated degradation behavior and (D) images comparison, (E) Degradation behavior under soil burial and (F) images comparison, and (G) Degradation mechanism of PLA composite films.
(A) TG curves, (B) DTG curves, (C) DSC curves, and (D) Thermal behavior mechanism of PLA composite films.

(A) Stress-strain curves, (B) Tensile modulus, (C) Tensile strength, (D) Elongations at break, (E) Images of tensile fracture, (F) Tensile properties comparison, and (G) Strengthening and toughening mechanism of PLA composite films.

head.ce:title: Achieving the simultaneous improvement of degradation, thermal, and mechanical properties of polylactic acid composite films by carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Engineering, Anhui Agricultural University, Hefei, 230036, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Engineering, Anhui Agricultural University, Hefei, 230036, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Biosystems Engineering and Food Science, Zhejiang University, Hangzhou, 310058, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Biosystems Engineering and Food Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Biosystems Engineering and Food Science, Zhejiang University, Hangzhou 310058, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Engineering, Anhui Agricultural University, Hefei, 230036, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Having porous structure, large surface area, and high carbon content of biochar facilitates interface bonding of polylactic acid (PLA) composites, but uneven dispersion by its irregular morphology is becoming a new challenge in damaging properties. Based on this, the novelty of this study is using carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to overcome the performance defects of caused PLA composites by biochar while the ultimate goal is to reveal the influence mechanism of CQDs on structure, characteristics, and properties of PLA composites based on disclosing the forming mechanism of CQDs. It was found that adding CQDs accelerated the degradation of PLA from the results of Phosphate Buffer Saline (PBS) degradation, hydrolysis, and soil degradation. PLA/CQDs composite films also showed better thermal properties due to the excellent thermal stability of CQDs, and nucleation effect of CQDs should be responsible for the improvement of PLA crystallization. Additionally, having good activity, regular morphology, and uniform size of CQDs facilitated uniform dispersion and good interface combination in PLA system and thereby improved the tensile strength, tensile modulus, and elongation at break simultaneously. As a comparison, the tensile strength, tensile modulus, and elongation at break of 1 wt% PLA/CQDs composite films are 55.00 MPa, 1.76 GPa, and 9.84 %, this provides a promising, sustainable, and eco-friendly solution for reinforcing PLA composites.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The production of green composites has become increasingly sort after due to the pollution from traditional petroleum-based materials and the increasing demand of social environmental protection [1]. In terms of catering to the ideal of sustainability, biocomposites are favored in various industrial applications. Biocomposite are usually obtained by blending and processing enhanced phase, continuous phase, and other auxiliaries [2]. Typical enhanced phases include but are not limited to natural plant fibers, industrial wastes, synthetic fibers, mineral fibers, etc., while polymer matrixes including polylactic acid (PLA), polyethylene (PE), polypropylene (PP), polyvinyl chloride (PVC), etc. are often used as continuous phases in biocomposites [3–8]. Having better properties and sustainability should handle the wider application of biocomposites in construction, furniture manufacturing, and transportation packaging showing great potential in reducing pollution and saving resources [9]. As a comparison with other continuous phases, PLA has unique characteristics due to its biodegradability, transparency, processability and biocompatibility [7,10–12]. PLA demonstrates unparalleled versatility and promise from eco-friendly packaging solutions to cutting-edge biomedical devices, which offers a compelling alternative to traditional petroleum-based plastics. However, the inherent problems of PLA such as brittleness, low thermal stability, and slow crystallization rate have become challenges for its application in many specific fields [10]. Integrating PLA with other additives to prepare PLA composites is an idea strategy for overcoming PLA drawbacks.

Conventional approach for modifying PLA has been revolving around the improvement of its strength or toughness, typically by means of blending with rigid and flexible additives. Recent studies reported the use of biochar in reinforcing PLA composites emphasizing efficiency specifically as it is stable, green, and environmentally friendly [6,13–15]. Biochar, as a type of biobased materials, is a renewable material rich in carbon which can be used as a filler to decrease the proportion of polymer matrix in composites, thus reducing production costs. Interestingly, biochar with rich porous structure, high carbon content, large specific surface area, and good strength can effectively strengthen the interface and properties of the polymer composites [16]. Possessing good properties and low cost of biochar composites shows great potential in composite industry while the greater attraction of them is replacing conventional petroleum-based fillers by sustainable biochar. Idrees et al. [17] incorporated biochar into poly (ethylene terephthalate) (PET) by 3D printing, and it can be concluded that the inclusion of biochar increased tensile strength by 32 % and Young's modulus by 60 %. In a recent study, high density polyethylene (HDPE) was reinforced with optimized biochar, the results showed that the tensile strength by over 20 % and flexural strength by 35.9 % were obtained by adding biochar compared to pure HDPE [4]. Our previous study also confirmed that adding biochar can improve the mechanical and thermal properties of PLA or HDPE. Biochar, especially pyrolysis biochar with the defects of uneven particle size and irregular morphology, fails to disperse in the polymer matrix uniformly and obtain stable performance improvement of biochar composites. Hence, biochar microsphere with even particle size and regular morphology obtained by hydrothermal method is also added to replace pyrolysis biochar to prepare polymer composites. The relevant reports showed that adding biochar microsphere can improve the strength and modulus of PLA or zein composites effectively, but the toughness of the composites was reduced, especially the composite films [18]. There is an urgent need to meet the challenge of biochar microsphere composites films.

Nanomaterials can achieve the synergistic strengthening and toughening of polymer composites due to the characteristics such as light weight, large specific surface area, excellent mechanical properties, and good size effect [19]. Various attempts involving adding nanomaterials have been done to improve the properties of polymer composites including carbon nanotubes [20], nanocellulose [14], nano silicon dioxide [21], nano montmorillonite [22], nano calcium carbonate [23], nano titanium dioxide [24], etc. Also, the composites with adding the mentioned nanomaterials exhibit good mechanical, thermal, and antibacterial properties and are indispensable in transportation, medicine, energy and other fields. Report showed that the nanocomposites prepared by montmorillonite, graphene oxide, and polyethylene glycol (PEG) exhibited higher adsorption efficiency aqueous contaminant uptake. Additionally, Lee et al. [25] synthesized floatable photocatalytic elastomer–hydrogel nanocomposites, the nanocomposites showed efficient light delivery, facile supply of water, and instantaneous gas separation. Despite the satisfying capabilities and interesting trait of these nanocomposites, the perennial problems of high cost, non-renewable, or non-degradable are still not be ignored.

In light of these, research attention has turned to nano-sized biochar as a potential alternative of the mentioned nanomaterials. As the typical nano-sized biochar, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are generally derived from biomass sources showing excellent degradability, renewability, and abundance feedstock sources [26,27]. The preparation methods of CQDs are generally classified as bottom-up methods such as hydrothermal synthesis, microwave-assisted synthesis and top-down methods such as laser ablation and electrochemical methods. The unique properties of CQDs including high surface area, tunable fluorescence, and biocompatibility make them attractive for various applications in fields such as optoelectronics, sensing, imaging, and biomedicine [26,28]. Additionally, CQDs have garnered increasing interest in enhancing mechanical, thermal, and electrical properties in various matrices such as polymers, ceramics, and metals, this successful application is expected to provide feasibility for the preparation of polylactic acid composites by using CQDs [29].

In this study, CQDs were prepared using wheat straw by hydrothermal process and PLA/CQDs composite films were prepared by solution casting method. Incorporating CQDs into PLA composites are anticipated to achieve excellent biodegradability, superior mechanical properties, and outstanding thermal performance. The main novelty of this study is to prepare PLA composites with excellent properties using CQDs in a sustainable way while the ultimate goal is to investigate the mechanism of CQDs in PLA system. The attempt of incorporating CQDs into PLA composites aims to and find a sustainable and environmentally friendly option for enhancing PLA composites and propose a promising strategy for developing high-performance and sustainable materials.

All chemicals and reagents used in this study were of analytical grade, and deionized (DI) water was prepared using a lab deionizer water machine. The wheat straw was sourced from a village in Anhui province, while trichloromethane (CHCl3) with a purity of 99.0 % was purchased from Wuxi Zhanwang Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ferric chloride (FeCl3) was obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Company, while PLA (3001D) with a density of 1.25 g/cm3 and a relative viscosity of 3.0–3.5 was sourced from NatureWorks LLC.

As shown in Fig. 1 (A), CQDs were synthesized using wheat straw powder as the precursor through hydrothermal carbonization method [28,30,31]. First, the wheat straw was dried, crushed, ground, and sifted to obtain particles with a particle size of approximately 160 μm. Then, 6 g wheat straw particles and 1.2 g FeCl3 were dissolved in 60 mL DI water under continuous stirring. The resulting solution was transferred into a 100 mL polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) lining-lined stainless autoclave and maintained at 180°C for 12 h. After cooling at room temperature for 6 h, the dark brown solution was obtained by filtering out the large solids using a 0.22 μm microporous filter membrane. The dark brown solution was dialyzed in a dialysis membrane (3500 MWCO) for 48 h, with DI water exchange every 6 h to remove impurities and catalyst FeCl3. Finally, the dialyzed solution was freeze-dried for 48 h to obtain CQDs.

As shown in Fig. 2 (A), PLA/CQDs composite films with varying CQDs loadings were prepared using the solution casting method [18]. Initially, 2 g, 1.99 g, 1.98 g, 1.97 g, and 1.96 g PLA pellets (dried at 55°C for 24 h) were completely dissolved in 25 mL CHCl3 respectively. Subsequently, CQDs with various masses (0, 0.01 g, 0.02 g, 0.03 g, and 0.04 g) were added to the corresponding PLA solution and stirred thoroughly. After sonicating for 30 min, the resulting PLA/CQDs solutions were poured into polytetrafluoroethylene molds. The molds containing the various PLA/CQDs solutions were placed in a vacuum drying oven for 48 h to obtain the final composite films. The composite films with 0, 0.5 wt%, 1 wt%, 1.5 wt%, or 2 wt% CQDs contents are named as PLA, PC0.5, PC1, PC1.5, and PC2 respectively.

The chemical functional groups of the CQDs were characterized by a Fourier infrared spectrometer (FTIR) (Nicolet IS50, Thermo Nicole, USA), the FTIR spectra were tested and obtained with the range of 4000 cm−1 to 400 cm−1 and a resolution of 4 cm−1 using KBr method. The CQDs were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) using a diffractometer equipped with a Cu target and Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å). The scanning range was set from 5° to 80° with a scanning speed of 2°/min. The chemical structure of CQDs was analyzed using a Raman spectrometer (LabRAM HR Evolution, Horiba-Jobin Yvon, France). A laser with a wavelength of 633 nm was employed covering a spectral scanning range of 100 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1 with an exposure time of 16 s.

The pyrolysis behavior of CQDs was investigated using a thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) (TG 209 F1, NETZSCH, Germany). The sample was heated from room temperature to 600°C at a rate of 10°C/min under a nitrogen atmosphere to determine the thermal stability and decomposition characteristics of CQDs. The fluorescence properties of CQDs were characterized. In detail, 0.01 g CQDs were completely dissolved in ethanol solution, and then the solution was fluorescently detected using the UV lamp with a wavelength of 395 nm. The excitation and emission spectra of CQDs were detected and recorded using fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan). The absorbance of CQDs was tested using a UV–Visible spectrophotometer (UV-2601, Beijing Beifen Ruili Analytical Instrument Group Limited Liability Company, China) with a range of 200–700 nm. The nano-microstructures of CQDs were studied using the Transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEM-2100F, JEOL, Japan). The CQDs samples were suspended in ethanol water solution and then evaporated in air, with suspension droplets on a porous carbon film supported by a copper grid finally the structure images of CQDs are observed by different magnifications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the PLA/CQDs composite films
ce:section.ce:para:
XRD analysis was also used to investigate the effect of CQDs on the crystal structure of PLA. A Cu target and Kα radiation source (wavelength of 1.54 Å) were used with a scanning range from 5° to 80° and a scanning speed of 2°/min. This analytical method can effectively reveal the impact of CQDs on the crystal structure of PLA. FTIR was also used to characterize the chemical functional groups of the PLA/CQDs composite films. The spectra were obtained using the KBr method covering a range from 4000 cm−1 to 400 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was employed to investigate the morphologies of PLA/CQDs composite films at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. To enhance conductivity for improved imaging, gold was sputtered onto the fracture surfaces of the samples prior to observation. The thermal properties of the PLA/CQDs composite films were analyzed using a differential scanning calorimeter (DSC) (214 Polyma, Netzsch Group, Germany). The samples were first heated to 180°C at 10°C/min and held for 3 min to remove thermal history. They were then cooled at the same rate to room temperature and reheated from room temperature to 180°C at 10°C/min. Throughout the process, nitrogen was purged at a flow rate of 50 mL/min, and the second heating cycle was recorded. Additionally, the pyrolysis behavior of the PLA/CQDs composite films was investigated using TGA. The surface contact angles of PLA/CQDs composite films were measured using a surface contact angle measurement instrument (OCA20, Dataphysics, Germany). Measurements were conducted at 23°C and 52 % relative humidity, and the left and right contact angle values were recorded at the 10th second to obtain the average for each sample. The water absorption capacity of the PLA/CQDs composite films was evaluated by immersing the samples in DI water at room temperature. Film samples with uniform dimensions were weighed to obtain their initial mass (W0). The samples soaked in DI water were wiped to remove water, weighed, and recorded for weight (W1). The water absorption percentage was calculated using the following formula:
Water
absorption
( % )
=
(
W 1
−
W 0
W 0
)
× 100

The in vitro degradation of PLA/CQDs composite films was assessed by immersing uniformly sized samples in 50 mL Phosphate Buffer Saline (PBS, pH 7.2) at 50°C. At regular intervals, the samples were removed, rinsed with DI water, dried, and weighed to calculate mass loss, providing a measure of degradation over time. Additionally, samples measuring approximately 30 mm × 40 mm were cut from the PLA/CQDs composite films, disinfected with 72 % (v/v) ethanol, and allowed to air dry. These samples were then buried in a container filled with garden soil at a depth of about 6 cm and incubated at room temperature. At specified intervals, the samples were retrieved and weighed to evaluate weight loss over time. Specifically, all the calculation methods were the same as those used for water absorption, and each sample was measured three times to calculate the average and error. The mechanical properties of the composite films were evaluated using a tensile testing machine (UTM4104X, Sunstest Co., Ltd., China). Tensile tests were conducted at a crosshead speed of 20 mm/min, with a stretch distance of 30 mm. Dumbbell-shaped specimens, precisely cut using a custom-designed cutting knife, were tested five times for each sample. The average values from these tests were recorded as the final results.

Fig. 1 illustrates the preparation process and the fundamental characterization of CQDs. As shown in Fig. 1(B), a broad absorption band is observed around 3400 cm−1, corresponding to O–H stretching vibrations, which indicates the hygroscopicity of CQDs. The peaks at around 2930 cm−1, 2166 cm−1, 1591 cm−1, 1371 cm−1, 1261 cm−1, 1060 cm−1, and 790 cm−1 are attributed to C–H stretching vibrations, C–H overtone vibrations, –NH2 stretching vibrations, –COOH stretching, C–O–C stretching vibrations, C–O stretching vibrations, C–H out-of-plane bending, the FTIR results of CQDs are consistent with other reports [32,33]. Fig. 1(C) presents the XRD pattern of CQDs. Two distinct diffraction peaks are observed within the 2θ range of 5°–80°. The peaks at 23.3° and 43.5° correspond to the (002) and (100) lattice planes of graphitic carbon, respectively [27,34], which are also consistent with the SAED pattern shown in Fig. S1(B). The large full-width at half maximum and low intensity of these peaks suggest that the CQDs exhibit a predominantly amorphous structure endowed by hydrothermal reaction. Fig. 1(D) presents the Raman spectrum of CQDs. It can be observed that there are two distinct diffraction peaks at 1350 cm−1 and 1570 cm−1, the two peaks are mainly contributed by the D and G bands. The two bands correspond to the absorption of the sp3 hybrid amorphous carbon and sp2 hybrid crystalline carbon, this suggests the presence of carbon core structure which is composed of sp3 and sp2 hybrid carbon [35,36]. It can be concluded from the results of FTIR, XRD and Raman that CQDs have typical amorphous structure, which may be attributed to the presence of numerous oxygen-containing functional group defects in the CQDs.

TGA including TG and DTG curves of CQDs is shown in Fig. 1(E). It can be observed that there are three stages in whole thermogravimetric process of CQDs. The slight weight loss at around 62°C can be attributed to the evaporation of free water. It should be noted that a sharp decrease from 290°C to 370°C in TG can be seen. The main volatile matter inside CQDs decomposed and precipitated which includes but are not limited to macromolecular chain breaking, ester bond recombination, chain crosslinking, etc. at this stage [37]. Finally, the TG curve stabilizes as the temperature exceeds 370°C indicating that most of the volatile material has been released and stable residues are left. Moreover, the maximum degradation rate occurred at around 354°C, this indicates better thermal stability of CQDs due to the presence of thermal stable hybrid carbon. The fluorescence characteristics in Fig. 1(F) and Fig. S3 show that the absorption peaks appeared from 250 nm to 350 nm suggest strong absorption in the ultraviolet region of CQDs. The emission intensity of CQDs first increases and then decreases with the increase of excitation wavelength from 310 nm to 420 nm and the maximum photoluminescence excitation (PLE) and the maximum fluorescence (PL) emission are 328 nm and 426 nm, which displays excitation dependent emission feature of CQDs. The TEM image of CQDs shown in Fig. 1(G), along with the HR-TEM image in Fig. S1(A) and the particle size distribution in Fig. S2, reveals a uniformly dispersed spherical morphology with an average particle size of 5.06 ± 1.29 nm. This result is generally consistent with the microstructure of CQDs reported in previous studies [30,38]. No obvious aggregation of CQDs particles with good effective surface passivation can be attributed to good solubility of CQDs in ethanol and water. Having uniform particle size, good dispersion, excellent nanoscale of CQDs is expected to improve the interface bonding and mechanical properties of the resulted composites. Fig. 1(H) shows the synthesis mechanism of CQDs under hydrothermal conditions. Cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin in straw were hydrolyzed into glucose, xylose, and phenols [31]. Subsequently, glucose and xylose were further dehydrated and aggregated to form carbon ring network structure which can also occur through the ferric chloride catalytic oxidation of phenols [39,40]. Then the stable carbon nuclei were formed by the hydrothermal carbonization of carbon ring network structure [31]. Finally, the carbon nuclei grew into CQDs smaller than 10 nm through further continued carbonization and cross-linking reactions.

Fig. 2(B) shows that the school emblem was covered with different PLA composite film samples to characterize the transparency. As can be observed that pure PLA film has excellent transparency compared with other samples. The transparency of PC0.5 and PC1 become slightly worse with increasing the CQDs content to 0.5 wt% and 1 wt%, which indicates that 0.5 wt% or 1 wt% CQDs can be well dispersed in the PLA matrix and thus cause less optical transmission interference. However, the transparency of the films decreases more significantly with increasing CQDs loading to 1.5 wt% or even 2 wt%. Having excess CQDs can be easily to cause agglomerate, destroy the uniformity, lead to more pronounced light scattering and absorption, and thus affect the transparency of the films [41]. Fig. 2(C) and (D) present the XRD patterns and FTIR spectrum to characterize the chemical structure of PLA, PC0.5, PC1, PC1.5, and PC2. As shown in Fig. 2(C), five diffraction peaks can be observed at around 12.4°, 14.7°, 16.5°, 19°, and 22.2° for all the samples, these peaks correspond to (001/103), (010), (200/110), (203), and (015) crystal planes respectively [13]. All the five diffraction peaks are derived from PLA, which suggests that the crystal structure of PLA was not changed by amorphous CQDs. It should be noted that adding CQDs increased the intensity of all the peaks showing opposite trend compared with biochar microspheres [13] or pyrolytic biochar [42]. Increasing PLA crystallization intensities by CQDs can be attributed to the improved interaction between the crystalline regions of PLA by adding CQDs [36]. Fig. 2(D) shows the main functional groups of the samples to characterize the reaction between CQDs and PLA. The peaks at around 2998 cm−1, 2920 cm−1, 2850 cm−1, 1753 cm−1, 1454 cm−1, 1360 cm−1, 1181 cm−1, and 1080 cm−1 can attributed to C–H asymmetric stretching vibration, –CH2 asymmetrical stretching vibration and –CH2 symmetrical stretching vibration, C–O stretching vibration, –CH2 bending vibration, –CH2 wagging vibration, C–O–C stretching vibration, and C–O stretching vibration [13,43]. All the functional groups are derived from PLA or CQDs, which indicates that no covalent bond was formed by chemical reaction between CQDs and PLA. The SEM images of the fracture surfaces of PLA, PC0.5, PC1, PC1.5, and PC2 are illustrated in Fig. 2(E) to characterize the effect of CQDs on the microstructure of PLA. PLA exhibits a smooth microstructure than others and the SEM image of PC0.5 shows that a small number of punctate CQDs are distributed in PLA matrix. The microstructure of PC1 exhibits a uniform distribution indicating that 1 wt% CQDs is suitable. A rougher microstructure can be observed in the SEM image of PC1.5 and the roughness were further aggravated shown in the SEM image of PC2, this can be because that the resulting agglomeration by superfluous CQDs destroyed the interface structure with the CQDs content exceeds 1.5 wt%. PC1 has the most stable and uniform interface structure compared with other samples, better mechanical properties thus can be expected in PC1. On the other hand, no cavitation left by CQDs can be seen after tensile fracture indicating that there is good interfacial compatibility and bonding between CQDs and PLA [44]. Also, Fig. 2(F) shows the interfacial bonding mechanism of PLA composite films, which highlights the interaction between CQDs and the polymer matrix. The oxygen-containing functional groups on CQDs, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, can form hydrogen bonds with the ester groups of PLA, promoting stronger interfacial adhesion. This can be responsible for the bonding enhancement between CQDs and PLA and a more cohesive interface at the molecular level of PLA composite films. Also, penetrating the PLA matrix by nanometer-sized CQDs can facilitate physical interactions, increase the interaction surface area, and improve the interface bonding of PLA composite films [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation property of the composite films
ce:section.ce:para: The results of Fig. 3 reveal the wettability, water absorption, and degradation behavior of PLA composite films. As illustrated in Fig. 3(A) and Fig. S4, the sessile drop method was used to measure the water contact angles of the composite film surfaces. The results show that the average contact angle decreased from 62° for pure PLA to 36.5° for PC2 as the CQDs content increased to 2 wt%, confirming the enhanced hydrophilicity of the composite films [7,31]. On the one hand, the oxygen-containing functional groups (e.g., hydroxyl, carboxyl) in CQDs can form hydrogen bonds with water molecules, leading to the increase of water affinity. On the other hand, the incorporation of CQDs may alter the surface roughness, thus increase contact area with water, and further promote water spreading. Also, Fig. 3(B) illustrates the water absorption behavior of PLA/CQDs composite films. It can be observed that the water absorption increased with increasing CQDs contents, which is basically consistent with the results of the contact angles. It should be noted that the water absorption of all samples first increased and then decreased with the increase of soaking time. The distinct upward trend in water absorption for all samples during the initial phase can be attributed to the increase and diffusion of internal water gaps immersion of the samples [8]. PC2 exhibits the highest absorption rate of 1.45 % at day 7, in stark contrast to the 0.44 % observed for pure PLA film. The water absorption decreased after 7 days, which indicates a saturation of water absorption. Notably, the water absorption of all the samples became negative after 14 days, which suggests structural alterations and the early stages of degradation of the composite films [45]. Fig. 3(C) and (D) show the PBS accelerated degradation behavior of the composite films. Firstly, the PBS degradation rate increased with the increase of CQDs contents, this suggests that adding CQDs accelerated the hydrolysis and chain break, and thus promoted the degradation of PLA [46,47]. Also, the PBS degradation rate of all the samples increased significantly with the increase of soaking time. After 96 h, the PBS degradation rate of PLA is 2.23 %, while the composite films exhibit higher degradation with the maximum degradation rate of 2.71 % in PC2. The degradation performance of PLA composite films under soil burial conditions is also presented in Fig. 3(E) and (F). As shown in Fig. 3(F) the appearance morphology of the composite films was affected by soil burial environment significantly. Fig. 3(E) demonstrates that the degradation rate of composite films is positively correlated with the content of CQDs suggesting the degradation promotion of CQDs, which is consistent with the PBS accelerated degradation results. In addition, the degradation rate of composite films is positively correlated with burial time. After 28 days, the degradation rate of PLA is 3.37 %, while the composite films exhibit higher degradation with the maximum degradation rate of 4.08 % in PC2. The degradation of the composite films in soil environment can be due to a fact that the water in soil environment obtained by the hydrophilicity of CQDs and the enzyme released by microorganism in soil environment accelerated the degradation of PLA matrix [12,48]. It can be concluded from the mentioned results that PLA degradation can be promoted by adding CQDs and the degradation regulation of PLA/CQDs composite films can be achieved.

The degradation mechanism of PLA composite films is illustrated in Fig. 3(G). Initially, the hydrogen bonds between PLA and CQDs in the composite films were broken by the influence of water. PLA was further depolymerized into lactide under the –OH/OH− from the environment and enzymes produced by microorganisms in soil. Then, the CO in lactide were broken down to be lactic acid molecules, meanwhile the CQDs were preliminarily hydrolyzed [45]. Finally, CQDs and lactic acid molecules were decomposed into carbon dioxide, water, and biochar. In the degradation process of PLA composite films, CQDs played a role in accelerating the degradation of PLA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal stability analysis of the composite films
ce:section.ce:para: The TG and DTG curves of PLA composite films are presented in Fig. 4 (A) and (B) to characterize the thermal stability and thermal decomposition behavior. As shown in the thermogravimetric process that the weight loss occurs at around 60°C for all the samples, this can be attributed to the loss of water and the break of hydrogen bonds. A wide thermal decomposition of pure PLA from 140°C to 240°C with the maximum decomposition rate at around 170°C can be observed, which is mainly due to the break of ester bond. Pure PLA also exhibits more intense weight loss at around 250°C–360°C with the maximum decomposition rate at around 340°C, this is because the of the decomposition lactide. It should be noted that the maximum decomposition rate at around 170°C of pure PLA is delayed to around 265°C for PC0.5, PC1, PC1.5, and PC2. Also, the maximum decomposition rate at around 340°C is delayed to around 390°C for PC0.5, PC1, PC1.5, and PC2, and the decomposition peak at around 355°C of PC0.5, PC1, PC1.5, and PC2 can be attributed to decomposition of CQDs shown in Fig. 1(E). It can be concluded that obvious delay of maximum decomposition rate temperature and higher residue content indicates better thermal stability of PLA composite films by adding CQDs. The thermal decomposition mechanism of PLA composite films shown in Fig. 4(D) illustrates that thermal energy initially weakens intermolecular interactions, including hydrogen bonding and van der Waals forces, leading to the separation of the CQDs from PLA [37]. As the temperature increased, PLA was depolymerized through backbiting reactions via lactone exchange, leading to the formation of lactide and oligomers as primary decomposition products. At higher temperatures, lactide and CQDs were further dehydrated and decomposed into CO2, CO, C2H4O, and biochar [43]. The DSC results for PLA and composite films with varying concentrations of CQDs are presented in Fig. 4(C) to reveal key thermal parameters including the glass transition temperature (Tg), cold crystallization temperature (Tcc), melting temperature (Tm), and degree of crystallinity (Xc). The Tg of PLA, PC0.5, PC1, PC1.5, and PC2 were 61.22°C, 61.13°C, 61.04°C, 60.92°C, and 60.77°C which slightly indicates that adding CQDs decreases molecular weight of PLA. The Tm of PLA, PC0.5, PC1, PC1.5, and PC2 were 165.08°C, 165.75°C, 165.51°C, 164.75°C, and 165.81°C, the continuous rise of Tm with increasing CQDs contents suggest the improvement of thermal stability by CQDs again. The Tcc of PLA, PC0.5, PC1, PC1.5, and PC2 were 130.31°C, 127.5°C, 125.66°C, 123.21°C, and 120.57°C, and their Xc were 4.14 %, 10.85 %, 15.42 %, 20.85 %, and 24.18 %. The decrease of Tcc and increase of Xc can be obtained by adding CQDs, this indicates that adding CQDs as nucleating agents can facilitate the crystallization of PLA. The thermal behavior mechanism of CQDs to PLA is also revealed in Fig. 4(D). On the one hand, the facilitation of CQDs to PLA crystallization increased the energy which is required to melt PLA, increased Tm, and thereby increased the thermal stability [49]. On the other hand, CQDs by the nucleation effect promoted the order, improved the grain structure, and accelerated the crystallization of PLA [8]. This enhanced crystallinity is expected to improve the mechanical and thermal properties of the PLA composites, as the crystalline regions provide greater resistance to deformation and thermal degradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tensile properties of the composite films
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 (A–E) illustrate the tensile properties of the composite film samples, including stress-strain curves, tensile strength, tensile modulus, elongation at break, and tensile fracture images, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5(E), the comparison of samples before and after tensile fracture reveals a flat and neat fracture interface, suggesting a relatively uniform stress distribution under the applied load. According to the stress-strain curves in Fig. 5(A), the strength and toughness first increased and then decreased with increase CQDs content, and PC1 shows the best tensile properties among all the samples. In detail, the tensile strength of PLA, PC0.5, PC1, PC1.5, and PC2 is 41.15 MPa, 46.76 MPa, 55.00 MPa, 44.03 MPa, and 39.81 MPa respectively. Compared with pure PLA film, the tensile strength was increased with 33.66 % by adding 1 wt% CQDs shown in PC1. On the one hand, the hydrogen bonding between PLA and CQDs achieve a better interface bonding, which can effectively resist fracture [7]. On the other hand, 1 wt% CQDs can be uniformly dispersed in the PLA matrix to form the stable interface structure, which can effectively prevent excessive deformation [41]. Besides, the tensile strength decreased sharply as the CQDs contents were added to 1.5 wt% or even 2 wt% as shown in Fig. 5(C). Excess CQDs were aggregated in the PLA matrix resulting in stress concentration, which can be responsible for the strength decrease of PC1 and PC2. The tensile modulus of PLA, PC0.5, PC1, PC1.5, and PC2 is 1.62 GPa, 1.71 GPa, 1.76 GPa, 1.64 GPa and 1.70 GPa respectively. Similarly, the tensile modulus was increased with 8.64 % by adding 1 wt% CQDs shown in PC1. This result indicates that adding 1 wt% CQDs can also improve the stiffness of PLA. The decrease of tensile modulus by adding excess CQDs also suggests that the resistance to deformation was weakened by the agglomeration [50]. The elongation at break of PLA, PC0.5, PC1, PC1.5, and PC2 is 5.06 %, 9.08 %, 9.84 %, 8.66 %, and 4.22 % respectively as shown in Fig. 5(D). The elongation at break of pure PLA film is only 5.06 % showing inherently large brittleness. The elongation at break was increased with 78.8 % and 94.5 % by adding 0.5 wt% CQDs or 1 wt% CQDs showing that the toughness can be improved by CQDs. As for the decrease by adding 1.5 wt% CQDs or 2 wt% CQDs, which confirms the negative effect of aggregate by excess CQDs on mechanical properties again. The tensile strength, tensile modulus, and elongation at break of PC1 were 55.00 MPa, 1.76 GPa, and 9.84 % respectively demonstrating the significant advantages of CQDs in enhancing the mechanical properties of PLA. As shown in Fig. 5(F), this improvement surpasses that achieved by other fillers, such as Napier nano whisker cellulose (NWCs) [6], P. lobata root microcrystalline cellulose (PRMCC) [8], graphene and Mn–Zn ferrite (GR-MZF) [51], bamboo and kenaf fiber (BF-KF) [52], and graphene oxide and carbon nanotubes (GO@CNT) [53]. In addition to these improvements, CQDs are also preferred over these fillers due to their unique sustainability, low cost, and simple processing requirements. The strengthening and toughening mechanism of CQDs to as depicted in Fig. 5(G) indicates that the incorporation of CQDs into the PLA matrix enhances mechanical strength through several synergistic effects. Firstly, the enhanced interfacial adhesion and bonding force obtained from hydrogen bonding between the CQDs and PLA chains facilitated efficient stress transfer and improved mechanical properties [8]. Secondly, CQDs served as nucleating agents promoted crystallization, optimized crystal structure, and reduced defects and thereby increased the mechanical properties of PLA [54]. Moreover, the stable structure by CQDs formed in PLA system can improve the toughness and strength of PLA by hindering the initiation and propagation of micro-cracks during tensile loading [55].

In this study, CQDs were synthesized using wheat straw by hydrothermal method and incorporated into PLA through the solution casting method to prepare PLA/CQDs composite films. The structure, characteristics, and properties of PLA/CQDs composite films were explored and revealed based on disclosing the forming mechanism of CQDs. The results showed that CQDs accelerated the degradation of PLA under hydrolysis, PBS immersion and soil burial conditions, and adding CQDs facilitated crystallization and improved thermal stability of PLA. Additionally, the mechanical properties of PLA first increased and then decreased as the increase of CQDs contents with optimal performance at a 1 wt% loading. The tensile strength, tensile modulus, and elongation at break of PC1 were 55.00 MPa, 1.76 GPa, and 9.84 % with the increases by 33.6 %, 8.6 %, and 94.6 % compared to pure PLA. These enhancements effectively address the inherent challenges of PLA, such as brittleness, slow degradation rates, and limited thermal performance, which positioned CQDs to be sustainable and efficient additives for PLA composites. The PLA/CQDs composite films with excellent degradation, thermal, and mechanical properties have great potential in packaging and biomedical devices.

Jianlong Chen: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Xinyuan Guo: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Rui Tan: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Mengde Huang: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Junchao Ren: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Weiwei Liu: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Conceptualization. Mingfeng Wang: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Conceptualization. Bin Li: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Conceptualization. Zhong Ma: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Conceptualization. Qingfa Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.
